title: Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling
summary: Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling retrieves prior skills in an original independent selector and state-ownership decision scenario and requires implementation, behavior, failure-state, and transfer evidence.
objectives: Apply css attribute presence value, css attribute token language, css attribute substrings, css data aria state in unfamiliar constraints.
Produce or audit a real independent selector and state-ownership decision artifact without happy-path-only reasoning.
Preserve earlier requirements while testing keyboard, narrow-width, invalid, and assistive-semantic states.

starterFiles.html: <!-- Continue the Volunteer Shift Deck attribute-state work here. -->
starterFiles.css: /* Continue the attribute-driven styling system here. */

steps.title: 01 · inspect 1: shift form type and requirement styling audit · quiz
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim survives the changed case? Scenario: independent selector and state-ownership decision.
steps.why: Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 1 in independent selector and state-ownership decision.

content.title: independent selector and state-ownership decision evidence
content.text: For Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling, choose the css attribute presence value claim that preserves the independent selector and state-ownership decision purpose, native behavior, and understandable relationships when presentation changes.

hints: Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling checkpoint 1: identify the user task and data relationship first.
Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling checkpoint 1: compare source, native behavior, and announced meaning.
Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling checkpoint 1: make the smallest repair that preserves earlier passing work.

options.text: Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 1: an attribute selector changes the underlying semantic state it matches.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 1: set truthful attributes in HTML or behavior logic, then use presence or exact-value selectors to reflect those facts visually.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 1: style a control with [required] and assume the browser now requires it.

steps.why: Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 2 in independent selector and state-ownership decision.

content.text: Order the independent selector and state-ownership decision work by dependency and verify that each phase creates evidence needed by the next.

hints: Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling checkpoint 2: identify the user task and data relationship first.
Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling checkpoint 2: compare source, native behavior, and announced meaning.

options.text: independent selector and state-ownership decision phase 2.1: document the attribute value grammar
independent selector and state-ownership decision phase 2.2: choose token hyphen-prefix exact or another operator
independent selector and state-ownership decision phase 2.3: declare case handling only when the value model permits it
independent selector and state-ownership decision phase 2.4: test whole partial adjacent mixed-case and language-subcode values

Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling checkpoint 3: compare source, native behavior, and announced meaning.
Mastery Exam: Attribute-Driven Styling checkpoint 3: make the smallest repair that preserves earlier passing work.

options.text: Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 3: substring selectors remain safe when filenames and URL formats change arbitrarily.

Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 3: document the stable convention, choose the narrowest substring operator, test format changes, and replace brittle inference with explicit state.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 3: use href*="pdf" because any URL containing those letters must be a PDF.

options.text: Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 4: changing generated content or color updates an ARIA state automatically.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 4: update state in the owning behavior, synchronize ARIA and data attributes, then use selectors as presentation and verify every transition.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 4: use a red background as the only source of closed status without changing DOM text or data.

options.text: Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 25: an attribute selector changes the underlying semantic state it matches.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 25: set truthful attributes in HTML or behavior logic, then use presence or exact-value selectors to reflect those facts visually.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 25: style a control with [required] and assume the browser now requires it.

options.text: Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 28: changing generated content or color updates an ARIA state automatically.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 28: update state in the owning behavior, synchronize ARIA and data attributes, then use selectors as presentation and verify every transition.
Independent selector and state-ownership decision case 28: use a red background as the only source of closed status without changing DOM text or data.
